FT_IC1.evidence: Uses prospectively collected data from the ABCD study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures social media time (hours per day) trajectories from baseline to year 2

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses estimated associations between social media trajectories and year 2 cognitive performance scores

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CI reported: ORRT β=-1.39 (95% CI -2.10 to -0.68), PSMT β=-2.03 (95% CI -2.91 to -1.15), PVT β=-2.09 (95% CI -2.78 to -1.41), total composite β=-0.85 (95% CI -1.30 to -0.40)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis; group-based trajectory modeling with multiple linear regression examining prospective association between social media trajectories and cognitive outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA (December 2, 2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Social media is the IV (independent variable), not the DV. Outcome is cognitive performance (non-SM outcome). Also includes cross-sectional design at year 2 comparing trajectory groups.
notes: Three trajectory groups emerged: no/very low use (57.6%), low increasing (36.6%), high increasing (5.8%). All comparisons relative to reference group. Strong evidence of smartphone/SM→non-SM association across multiple cognitive domains.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data across 21 study sites, October 2016 to October 2018 baseline, ages 9-10 years; analyzed through 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures time spent on social media (hours per day) self-reported at baseline to 3-year follow-up

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal cross-lagged structural equation panel models assessing reciprocal associations between social media use and depressive symptoms across 4 waves with inferential tests

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CI reported: year 1 to year 2 β=0.07 (95% CI 0.01-0.12, p=.01); year 2 to year 3 β=0.09 (95% CI 0.04-0.14, p<.001); between-person β=-0.01 (95% CI -0.04 to 0.02, p=.46)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis; prospective cohort study with longitudinal cross-lagged structural equation modeling examining within-person and between-person associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA Network Open (May 21, 2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports both within-person and between-person associations; includes cross-sectional analysis (within-wave contemporaneous associations at year 3); also includes smartphone/SM→non-SM outcome (social media use→depressive symptoms). These conditions exclude FT-EC7 applicability.
notes: Used random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) to separate between-person from within-person effects. Found unidirectional within-person association: social media use predicts depressive symptoms, but not vice versa. Between-person differences not associated after accounting for covariates.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed prospective cohort data from ABCD Study (N=9,243); ages 10-11 years in Year 1 (2017-2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures typical daily time spent on six different screen subtypes including social media, texting, videos, video games; also total screen time

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses determining associations between typical daily screen time (Year 1) and manic symptoms (Year 3), adjusting for confounders. Also mediation analyses using generalized structural equation models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple B coefficients with 95% CI reported: overall screen time B=0.05 (95% CI 0.03, 0.07, p<0.001); social media B=0.20 (95% CI 0.09, 0.32, p=0.001); texting B=0.18 (95% CI 0.08, 0.28, p<0.001); videos B=0.14 (95% CI 0.08, 0.19, p<0.001); video games B=0.09 (95% CI 0.04, 0.14, p=0.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis; prospective cohort study examining associations between Year 1 screen time and Year 3 manic symptoms with mediation testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (February 19, 2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time/social media is the IV (independent variable), not the DV. Primary outcome is manic symptoms (non-SM outcome). Paper also reports mediation pathways, further precluding FT-EC7 applicability.
notes: Prospective associations found between overall screen time and four subtypes (social media, texting, videos, video games) with manic symptoms. Problematic social media use, video game use, and sleep duration identified as partial mediators (47.7%, 58.0%, and 9.0% mediation, respectively). Television and video chatting not significant predictors.
